cards.question: State which intermolecular force is responsible for the unusual properties of water.
cards.answer: Hydrogen bonding (between the δ+ H of one molecule and a lone pair on the δ− O of another).
cards.explanation: Each water molecule can form up to two hydrogen bonds via its two O–H bonds and two lone pairs.

cards.question: Explain why ice is less dense than liquid water.
cards.answer: In ice, the hydrogen bonds hold the water molecules in an open, regular lattice with relatively large gaps between molecules. This holds the molecules further apart than in liquid water, so the same mass occupies a larger volume — ice is less dense and floats.
markPoints: hydrogen bonds hold molecules in an open/regular lattice
molecules held further apart / larger gaps than in liquid
cards.explanation: Open hydrogen-bonded lattice = bigger volume per molecule = lower density.

cards.question: Explain why water has a relatively high boiling point compared with other simple molecular hydrides such as H₂S.
cards.answer: Water molecules form hydrogen bonds, which are stronger than the permanent dipole–dipole/London forces in H₂S. More energy is needed to overcome these hydrogen bonds, giving water a higher boiling point.
markPoints: water has hydrogen bonding (H₂S does not)
more energy needed to break the stronger hydrogen bonds
cards.explanation: It is the hydrogen bonds (not the covalent O–H bonds) that are overcome when water boils.
